required.title: [Tool Kit] A basic home repair toolkit

suggested.title: [Video Series] YouTube: This Old House — "How To" playlist
[Video Series] YouTube: Home Mender — plumbing and repair basics

competence_markers.description: The student can explain how the plumbing supply and DWV systems work in a residential home
The student can unclog a drain, fix a running toilet, and address a dripping faucet without assistance
The student can safely replace an outlet, switch, or light fixture with verified power-off procedure
The student can change an HVAC filter and program a thermostat
The student can patch drywall, apply caulk, and replace weatherstripping to a functional standard
The student can perform basic maintenance on all major household appliances
The student follows and maintains a household maintenance schedule
The student can accurately determine when a problem requires professional help versus a DIY fix
The student has completed at least twenty documented repairs or maintenance tasks
The student can use all tools in a standard home repair toolkit safely and appropriately

cross_connections.description: 2.3.3-B builds the generalist; 2.2 develops deep trade competence. The student who excels here may find a pathway into 2.2 specialization.
Every repair you do yourself saves money. Every deferred maintenance item is hidden debt. Home maintenance has direct financial consequences.
A clean home reveals maintenance issues that a dirty home hides. Cleaning and maintenance are complementary disciplines.
Caulk types, paint chemistry, cleaning solutions, plumbing solvents — applied chemistry is embedded throughout home maintenance.
Repairs done to a proper standard last. Repairs done "good enough" fail again. Hold yourself to the standard of doing it right.
Emergency response skills (burst pipe, power failure, furnace failure in winter) overlap with survival competencies. The home is the first shelter.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned that your home is a machine with systems — plumbing, electrical, HVAC, structural — and you built the habit of noticing when something seems wrong. You know where the breaker box is, where the water shutoff is, and what basic tools look like. That was observation. Now you are going to learn how to do the work.

The difference between someone who calls a plumber every time a toilet runs and someone who fixes it in ten minutes is not talent. It is knowledge and practice. A running toilet is almost always a flapper valve — a rubber piece that costs three dollars and takes five minutes to replace. A tripped breaker requires walking to the panel and flipping a switch. A clogged drain usually needs a plunger and five minutes of effort. A loose door hinge needs a screwdriver and thirty seconds.

These are not advanced skills. They are the basic operational competencies of someone who lives in a house. The person who cannot perform them is not saving time by calling a professional — they are paying a hundred dollars or more for something they could have done themselves, and they are dependent on someone else's schedule and availability for the basic functioning of their own home.

By the end of this packet, you will be able to perform the twenty most common household repairs and maintenance tasks. You will understand how the major systems in a home actually work — not at an engineering level, but at a practical, functional level that lets you diagnose problems, perform basic fixes, and know when a problem genuinely requires professional help.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Functional understanding of residential plumbing** — how water supply and drain systems work; ability to perform common plumbing repairs
2. **Functional understanding of residential electrical systems** — how circuits, breakers, outlets, and switches work; safe practices and the limits of DIY electrical work
3. **Functional understanding of HVAC systems** — how heating and cooling work; filter changes, thermostat management, recognizing system failures
4. **Structural repair competence** — patching drywall, caulking, weatherstripping, painting, tightening hardware, adjusting doors and windows
5. **Appliance maintenance skills** — cleaning, basic diagnostics, and maintenance of major household appliances
6. **A working maintenance schedule** — the student knows what needs attention monthly, quarterly, seasonally, and annually
7. **Tool competence** — confident, safe use of a standard home repair toolkit
8. **Diagnostic thinking** — the ability to observe a problem, identify its likely cause, and determine whether it is a DIY fix or a professional call
content.core_content:
### Part 1: How Plumbing Works

Your home's plumbing is two separate systems that share the same pipes but do different jobs.

**The supply system** brings clean water into the house under pressure. Water enters through a main line from the city water supply or a well pump. It passes through the water meter (which measures how much you use) and the main shutoff valve (which can stop all water flow to the house). From there, it branches through pipes to every fixture: sinks, toilets, showers, bathtubs, dishwashers, washing machines, and outdoor faucets.

One branch goes to the water heater, which heats the water and sends it through a parallel set of hot water pipes. This is why every fixture has two supply lines — hot and cold.

**The drain-waste-vent (DWV) system** removes used water and waste from the house using gravity. When you drain a sink or flush a toilet, the water flows downhill through drain pipes, through a trap (the U-shaped pipe under every sink — it holds water that blocks sewer gas from coming back up into the house), and into the main sewer line or septic system.

Vent pipes run from the drain system up through the roof. These let air into the system so water can flow freely — the same principle as putting a second hole in a can to pour liquid smoothly. Without vents, drains would gurgle, run slowly, and the traps could be siphoned dry.

**Common plumbing problems you will learn to fix:**

- **Clogged drains.** The most common plumbing problem. A sink drain is usually clogged by hair, soap, or food debris caught in the trap or just past it. A toilet is usually clogged by too much paper or something that should not have been flushed. Your tools: a plunger (use a flange plunger for toilets, a cup plunger for sinks), a drain snake (a flexible metal cable you feed into the pipe to break up or retrieve the clog), and for sink drains, removing and cleaning the P-trap.
- **Running toilet.** A toilet that keeps running after flushing is wasting water constantly. The cause is almost always one of three things: the flapper valve is worn and not seating properly (replace it — three dollars, five minutes), the fill valve is malfunctioning (replace it — ten dollars, twenty minutes), or the float is set too high (adjust it — zero dollars, two minutes). Lift the tank lid, flush, and watch. The mechanism is simple and visible.
- **Dripping faucet.** A faucet that drips when turned off usually needs a new washer, O-ring, or cartridge depending on the faucet type. Turn off the water supply to that fixture first. Disassemble the faucet handle, identify the worn part, take it to a hardware store to match it, and install the replacement. This is a twenty-minute repair that stops a problem costing you gallons of water per day.
- **Shutting off water.** Every fixture has its own shutoff valve — the small valve on the wall below or behind it. The main shutoff stops all water to the house. You must be able to locate and operate every shutoff in your home. In an emergency (burst pipe, major leak), the main shutoff is the first thing you reach.

### Part 2: How Electrical Systems Work

**Respect electricity. It can kill you.** This is not an exaggeration. Household current (120V in North America) is more than sufficient to cause fatal electrocution. Every electrical task you perform must begin with turning off the circuit at the breaker and verifying that the power is off with a voltage tester. No exceptions. Ever.

**How the system works:** Electricity enters your home from the utility company's lines through a service entrance and into the main electrical panel (breaker box). The panel distributes power through individual circuits — each protected by a circuit breaker. Each circuit serves a specific area or set of outlets in your home.

A circuit breaker trips (shuts off) when a circuit is overloaded — drawing more current than it can safely carry — or when there is a short circuit (an unintended path for electricity, which generates dangerous heat). A tripped breaker is a safety feature, not a malfunction. To reset it: find the tripped breaker (it will be in the middle position or slightly off), push it fully to the OFF position, then push it to ON. If it trips again immediately, there is an active problem on that circuit — do not keep resetting it. Find what is overloading the circuit or call an electrician.

**GFCI outlets** (Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters) — the outlets with TEST and RESET buttons, required near water sources (kitchens, bathrooms, outdoors) — shut off power instantly if they detect current flowing through an unintended path (like through your body). Test them monthly by pressing TEST, verifying the power stops, then pressing RESET.

**What you can safely do:**
- Reset a tripped breaker
- Replace a light switch (power OFF, verified with a tester)
- Replace an outlet (power OFF, verified with a tester)
- Replace a light fixture (power OFF, verified with a tester)
- Test and reset GFCI outlets
- Replace light bulbs and understand bulb types (incandescent, CFL, LED, wattage ratings)
- Identify a circuit by switching breakers off one at a time and checking which outlets lose power (label your panel)

**What requires a licensed electrician:**
- Any work in the main panel
- Adding new circuits
- Running new wiring
- Any work that involves the service entrance
- Anything you are not confident about — when in doubt, do not touch it

### Part 3: How HVAC Systems Work

HVAC stands for Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning. Your home's HVAC system maintains temperature and air quality.

**Forced-air systems** (the most common in North America) use a furnace or heat pump to heat air and an air conditioner or heat pump to cool it. A blower fan pushes conditioned air through ducts to vents in each room. Return vents pull air back to the system to be filtered and conditioned again.

**The filter is the most important maintenance item in your home.** The HVAC filter captures dust, pollen, pet dander, and debris before they enter the system. A dirty filter:
- Forces the system to work harder, increasing energy costs
- Reduces airflow, making some rooms too hot or cold
- Shortens the system's lifespan
- Degrades indoor air quality

Change the filter every one to three months depending on the type, whether you have pets, and your air quality. This takes less than five minutes and costs a few dollars. Write the filter size on the furnace with a marker so you always buy the correct size.

**Thermostat operation:** A programmable thermostat saves money by reducing heating and cooling when you are asleep or away. Set it and leave it. Constantly adjusting the thermostat wastes energy — the system does not heat or cool faster when you set it to an extreme temperature. It just runs longer.

**Signs of HVAC problems that need professional attention:**
- Unusual noises (banging, squealing, grinding)
- System running constantly without reaching the set temperature
- Uneven heating or cooling across rooms (could be duct issues)
- Water pooling around the unit
- Strange smells when the system runs
- Ice on the outdoor AC unit
- Carbon monoxide detector alarming (leave the house immediately and call 911)

### Part 4: Structural Repairs and Maintenance

The structure of your home — walls, floors, ceilings, doors, windows, roof — requires ongoing attention. Small problems left untreated become expensive problems.

**Drywall patching.** Holes in walls from door handles, furniture, removed picture hangers, or accidents are among the most common structural repairs. Small holes (nail holes to golf-ball sized) are patched with spackle or lightweight joint compound, applied with a putty knife, dried, sanded smooth, and painted. Larger holes require a drywall patch kit — a piece of drywall or a metal patch that bridges the hole, covered with joint compound, taped, sanded, and painted. Practice on a scrap piece first.

**Caulking.** Caulk is a flexible sealant used to fill gaps — around bathtubs, sinks, windows, door frames, and anywhere water or air might penetrate. Old caulk cracks, pulls away, and fails. Re-caulking is essential maintenance. Remove old caulk with a utility knife or caulk removal tool. Clean and dry the surface. Apply new caulk with a caulk gun in a smooth, continuous bead. Smooth it with a wet finger or caulk tool. Silicone caulk for wet areas (bathrooms, kitchens). Paintable latex caulk for windows and trim.

**Weatherstripping.** Doors and windows have weatherstripping — strips of rubber, foam, or vinyl that seal gaps when closed. Worn weatherstripping allows drafts, moisture, and insects to enter. Inspect it annually. Replace it when it is compressed, torn, or no longer makes a good seal. Types include adhesive-backed foam tape (easiest), V-strip (most durable), and door sweeps (bottom of exterior doors).

**Painting.** Interior painting is one of the highest-impact, lowest-cost improvements you can make. The skill is in preparation, not in the painting itself. Sand, clean, prime if necessary, tape edges, cut in with a brush, and roll the large areas. Use quality paint and good rollers. Two coats minimum. Clean up immediately. You should be able to paint a room to a professional-looking finish.

**Door adjustments.** Doors stick, sag, and squeak. A sticking door usually needs its hinges tightened (screws work loose over time). If the screw holes are stripped, remove the screw, insert a wooden toothpick or golf tee with glue into the hole, let it dry, and re-drive the screw. A squeaky hinge needs a drop of oil or silicone lubricant. A door that does not latch may need its strike plate adjusted.

**Window maintenance.** Check windows annually for: cracked or missing caulk around frames, damaged weatherstripping, condensation between double-pane glass (indicating seal failure), hardware that does not lock properly. Clean window tracks with a vacuum and stiff brush.

### Part 5: Appliance Maintenance

Major appliances are expensive to replace and surprisingly simple to maintain. Most appliance failures are caused by neglected maintenance, not manufacturing defects.

**Refrigerator:** Clean the condenser coils (underneath or behind the unit) every six to twelve months. A vacuum with a brush attachment works. Dirty coils make the compressor work harder and fail sooner. Check door seals — close the door on a dollar bill. If you can pull it out easily, the seal needs replacing. Keep the temperature at 35-38 degrees Fahrenheit (1.5-3.5 Celsius).

**Washing machine:** Check supply hoses for bulging, cracking, or leaking. Replace rubber hoses with braided stainless steel hoses — a rubber hose failure causes catastrophic flooding. Clean the lint trap (top-loaders) and run a cleaning cycle monthly (front-loaders) to prevent mold and odor. Leave front-loader doors open between uses.

**Dryer:** Clean the lint trap before every load. This is not optional — lint buildup is a leading cause of house fires. Once a year, disconnect the vent hose and clean the entire vent run from dryer to exterior. Check that the exterior vent flap opens freely.

**Dishwasher:** Clean the filter (bottom of the unit) monthly. Run an empty cycle with white vinegar quarterly. Check the door seal for buildup. Ensure the spray arms spin freely and their holes are not clogged.

**Water heater:** Flush the tank annually to remove sediment that reduces efficiency and capacity. Attach a garden hose to the drain valve at the bottom, run it to a floor drain or outside, and open the valve until the water runs clear. Check the temperature setting — 120 degrees Fahrenheit (49 Celsius) is the standard recommendation; higher wastes energy and increases scald risk. Check the pressure relief valve annually by lifting the lever briefly — water should flow and stop when released.

### Part 6: The Maintenance Schedule

A functioning household runs on a maintenance schedule, not on crisis response. Here is your framework:

**Monthly:**
- Check HVAC filter (replace if dirty)
- Test smoke detectors and CO detectors
- Test GFCI outlets
- Run water in infrequently used fixtures (keeps traps from drying out)
- Check under sinks for leaks
- Clean dishwasher filter
- Inspect dryer lint trap and area around dryer for lint buildup

**Quarterly:**
- Run dishwasher cleaning cycle
- Clean range hood filter
- Check water softener salt level (if applicable)
- Test garage door auto-reverse safety feature
- Check caulk around tubs and showers

**Semi-annually:**
- Clean refrigerator coils
- Inspect washing machine hoses
- Check weatherstripping on exterior doors
- Flush water heater (annually at minimum)
- Clean dryer vent run
- Inspect exterior — gutters, downspouts, foundation, siding

**Annually / Seasonally:**
- Change HVAC filter schedule review (adjust based on conditions)
- Professional HVAC tune-up (fall for heating, spring for cooling)
- Inspect roof (from ground with binoculars or safely from a ladder)
- Clean gutters (fall after leaves drop, spring after pollen)
- Check attic for signs of leaks, pests, or insulation issues
- Winterize outdoor faucets (shut off interior valve, open outdoor faucet to drain)
- Check and replace smoke detector and CO detector batteries (or units if they are ten years old)
- Inspect fire extinguisher (check pressure gauge, ensure pin is intact)
- Touch up interior and exterior paint as needed

### Part 7: The DIY Decision — When to Fix It and When to Call a Professional

Not every problem is a DIY project. Knowing the boundary is as important as knowing how to fix things.

**Always call a professional for:**
- Gas line work (gas leaks can be fatal — if you smell gas, leave the house and call the gas company)
- Main electrical panel work
- Structural modifications (load-bearing walls, foundation issues)
- Roof replacement
- Major plumbing re-routes
- HVAC refrigerant work (requires certification)
- Anything involving permits (additions, major renovations)
- Anything you have researched and still do not feel confident about

**You can handle:**
- Most clogged drains
- Running toilets
- Dripping faucets
- Tripped breakers
- Replacing outlets, switches, and light fixtures (with power off and verified)
- HVAC filter changes
- Drywall patches
- Caulking and weatherstripping
- Painting
- Door and window adjustments
- Basic appliance maintenance
- Replacing supply hoses

**The decision framework:** Can I do this safely? Do I understand what I am doing well enough to do it correctly? Is the cost of doing it wrong greater than the cost of hiring a professional? If you answer no to any of these, hire someone. There is no shame in hiring a professional for work that exceeds your skill level. The shame is in not being able to handle the basics.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Plumbing Competence
**Developing well:** The student can explain how supply and drain systems work, can unclog drains confidently, has fixed a running toilet, and knows how to shut off water at the fixture and main levels. They approach plumbing problems with the assumption they can fix them.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite how plumbing works but hesitates to actually work on it. They have not completed the hands-on plumbing tasks. They defer to you for any plumbing issue.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student actually had their hands on plumbing fixtures? Book knowledge without practice creates false confidence or persistent hesitation. Assign a specific plumbing repair and stand back.

### 2. Electrical Safety and Competence
**Developing well:** The student's first action before any electrical work is to turn off the breaker and test with a voltage tester. This is automatic. They have replaced an outlet or switch successfully and can explain what they did and why. They know the clear line between DIY electrical work and professional electrical work.

**Developing slowly:** The student is either cavalier about electrical safety (dangerous) or paralyzed by fear of electricity (limiting). Either extreme needs addressing.

**Diagnostic questions:** For the cavalier student: quiz them on exactly what happens when you touch a live wire. Make the danger concrete. For the fearful student: walk them through a simple switch replacement step by step, with verified power off. Competent fear management comes from supervised practice, not from avoidance.

### 3. Maintenance Schedule Adherence
**Developing well:** The student has created a maintenance calendar, follows it, and checks off completed tasks. They are beginning to notice maintenance needs proactively — "The filter is due for a change" rather than waiting for a reminder.

**Developing slowly:** The calendar exists on paper but is not being followed. Tasks are done only when you assign them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the calendar posted somewhere visible? Is there a regular check-in (weekly) where the student reviews what is due? The system needs to be designed for actual use, not just for documentation.

### 4. Diagnostic Thinking
**Developing well:** When something goes wrong, the student's first response is to investigate: What are the symptoms? What system is involved? What are the most likely causes? They can walk through a diagnostic process and reach a reasonable conclusion about whether to fix it themselves or call a professional.

**Developing slowly:** The student either jumps to action without diagnosing (fixing the wrong thing) or immediately declares the problem beyond their ability without investigating.

**Diagnostic questions:** Present the student with a hypothetical: "You come home and the kitchen light does not turn on. Walk me through what you check." A competent student checks the bulb, checks the switch, checks the breaker, checks if other things on that circuit work — a systematic process of elimination.
